title: Being a FAANG Interviewer — How to Hire & How to Evaluate
description: Staff+ engineers at FAANG spend 10-20% of their time interviewing. How to design effective interview questions, write calibration rubrics, give feedback that helps hiring committees, sit on HC panels, avoid bias, and evaluate SAP/enterprise candidates fairly. Includes the shadowing progression to become a certified interviewer and the specific challenges of evaluating engineers from nontraditional backgrounds.

sections.title: Why Interviewing Is a Staff+ Responsibility

description.value:
At FAANG, interviewing is not optional for Staff+ engineers. It is a core responsibility, weighted equally with technical delivery in performance reviews. The rationale: Staff+ engineers define the engineering culture. Who you hire determines what the organization becomes. A bad hire costs the company $500K-$2M in salary, onboarding, and opportunity cost. A good hire generates 10x that in value. The Staff+ interviewer is the gatekeeper.

Interviewing as a Staff+ multiplier: (1) Scale through hiring: One great hire contributes more value than you can personally deliver in the same time. Every hour you spend interviewing that results in a strong hire is highly leveraged. (2) Shape the culture: The engineers you hire will influence the team's norms, standards, and decision-making for years. A single bad hire can damage team culture. (3) Build your reputation: Interviewers who consistently identify strong candidates are valued by the organization. Your interview feedback becomes trusted. You are invited to join the hiring committee. (4) Learn from candidates: Interviewing exposes you to different approaches, technologies, and perspectives. Every candidate teaches you something.

SAP Bridge: In SAP consulting, hiring is typically done by HR and project managers. Technical interviews are rare and usually focus on certification checks and experience verification. FAANG's hiring process is engineer-driven: Staff+ engineers design the questions, conduct the interviews, and make the hiring decisions. This is a significant responsibility that SAP engineers are not prepared for. The good news: your SAP experience with evaluating team members on complex enterprise projects translates directly to evaluating candidates for FAANG roles.

sections.title: Designing Effective Interview Questions

description.value:
A good interview question is not just technically interesting. It is calibrated to differentiate between levels, resistant to memorization, and produces consistent signals across candidates. Designing such questions is a skill that requires practice and peer review.

Characteristics of good interview questions: (1) Multiple entry points: A candidate at L4 can produce a working solution. An L5 candidate can discuss tradeoffs and optimizations. An L6 candidate can connect the problem to real-world systems and design for scale. The question should have depth that reveals the candidate's level naturally. (2) Open-ended but scoped: The problem is realistic but not undefined. 'Design a URL shortener' is too vague. 'Design a URL shortener that handles 100M URLs per month with 99.99% availability' is scoped enough to evaluate. (3) Resistant to memorization: Avoid questions that are widely published (e.g., 'Design Twitter,' 'Design Uber'). Candidates can memorize the answer. Instead, introduce a twist: 'Design Twitter for a government client that requires 100% data sovereignty and zero data leaving the country.' (4) Productive for the candidate: The question should give the candidate opportunities to demonstrate their strengths, not just expose weaknesses. A good question makes the candidate feel they performed well regardless of outcome.

Level differentiation through question design: (1) L4/L5 question: Focus on implementation, data structures, API design. Example: 'Design a rate limiter for a REST API. The rate limiter should support configurable limits per user.' (2) L5/L6 question: Add distributed systems concerns (consistency, partitioning, fault tolerance). Example: 'Design a rate limiter for a globally distributed API. The rate limiter must be consistent across regions within 5 seconds.' (3) L6+ question: Add organizational concerns (multiple teams, migration strategy, operational excellence). Example: 'Design a rate limiter for an API that is managed by 5 different teams. Each team has different rate limits, and you must migrate from the current per-instance rate limiter to the new global one without downtime.'

sections.codeBlock:
# Interview Question Design Template

## Question: [Name]

### Target Level: [L4/L5/L6+]

### Description
[2-3 sentence problem statement with constraints]

### What L4 Looks Like
- Produces a working solution
- Uses appropriate data structures
- Handles basic edge cases

### What L5 Looks Like
- Discusses tradeoffs between approaches
- Handles error cases and failure modes
- Considers performance characteristics

### What L6+ Looks Like
- Connects the problem to real-world systems
- Discusses operational concerns (monitoring, deployment, rollback)
- Considers organizational impact (multiple teams, migration strategy)
- Identifies and mitigates risks

### Must-Have Signals
[The specific behaviors/answers that indicate the candidate should advance]

### Red Flags
[Answers or behaviors that indicate the candidate should not advance]

### Common Candidate Mistakes
[Anticipated errors and how to evaluate them]

scenarios.choice: A: Easier question with more time for depth and follow-ups
scenarios.tradeOff: +Most candidates can reach a working solution, giving you more data on how they think but -May not differentiate L4 from L5 well (both can solve it, difference is in discussion depth)
scenarios.context: You ask a medium-difficulty coding problem that 80% of candidates can solve in 25 minutes. You spend the remaining 20 minutes asking follow-ups about scaling, edge cases, and production concerns. The candidate who finishes quickly gets more depth.
scenarios.recommendation: Best for phone screens and early-round interviews where the goal is to filter out weak candidates. Use this format when you want to see how the candidate thinks when they have a working solution.

scenarios.choice: B: Harder question that most candidates will not fully solve
scenarios.tradeOff: +Strong level differentiation (L6 candidates solve it, L4 candidates struggle) but -Can leave good candidates feeling like they failed even when they are strong enough for the role
scenarios.context: You ask a hard system design problem that requires deep distributed systems knowledge. Only 20% of candidates reach an optimal solution. The other 80% get partial credit. The candidate leaves the interview feeling they failed.
scenarios.recommendation: Use this format only for on-site rounds where the candidate has other rounds to demonstrate their strengths. Never make all rounds hard — the candidate needs at least one round where they feel successful to perform at their best.

sections.title: Writing Effective Interview Feedback

description.value:
Interview feedback is the single most important artifact in the hiring process. The hiring committee (HC) does not interview the candidate. They read the feedback. If your feedback is vague, unsubstantiated, or inconsistent, the HC cannot make an informed decision. Writing effective feedback is a skill that distinguishes great interviewers from average ones.

Structure of effective feedback: (1) Summary (1-2 sentences): Your overall recommendation and confidence level. 'Strong Hire. The candidate demonstrated L6-level system design depth and cross-team thinking.' (2) Evidence (3-5 bullet points): Specific, observable behaviors. Not 'the candidate was good at system design' but 'the candidate identified the data consistency requirement as the key constraint, discussed three consistency models (strong, eventual, causal), and chose causal consistency with a clear rationale based on the payment reconciliation use case.' (3) Level assessment: What level did the candidate demonstrate? What evidence supports this assessment? 'L6 — the candidate connected the design to organizational concerns (migration strategy, team dependencies, operational excellence). This is beyond L5 scope.' (4) Red flags or concerns: Any specific concerns and their severity. 'Minor concern: the candidate initially suggested a single database for all regions. When probed, they identified the latency issue and proposed a multi-region approach. This is acceptable for L6 — the key is they corrected when prompted.'

Common feedback mistakes: (1) Halo effect: One strong answer leads to positive bias across all signals. 'The candidate was excellent at coding, so their system design must also be strong.' Calibrate each signal independently. (2) The 'nice person' bias: The candidate was friendly and likeable, so you recommend hire. Friendliness is not a hiring signal. (3) Vague praise: 'Good communication skills.' What specifically was good? 'The candidate structured their system design presentation clearly: requirements first, then estimation, then data model, then architecture, then deep dives.' (4) Vague criticism: 'Could improve coding.' What specifically? 'The candidate solved the problem but used O(n^2) algorithm when O(n log n) was achievable. They did not discuss time complexity unless prompted.'

sections.codeBlock:
# Interview Feedback Template

## Round: [Coding/System Design/Behavioral]

## Recommendation: [Strong Hire / Hire / Weak Hire / No Hire / Strong No Hire]

## Evidence
1. [Specific behavior]: [What the candidate did]
2. [Specific behavior]: [What the candidate did]
3. [Specific behavior]: [What the candidate did]

## Level Assessment
- Demonstrated level: [L4/L5/L6+]
- Evidence: [Specific behaviors that support this level]

## Concerns
- [Concern and severity]: [Context and whether the candidate recovered]

## Signal Summary
- Coding: [Strong / Acceptable / Weak]
- System Design: [Strong / Acceptable / Weak]
- Communication: [Strong / Acceptable / Weak]
- Problem Solving: [Strong / Acceptable / Weak]

adversarialResponse.challenge: "I tend to give everyone the benefit of the doubt. If a candidate is close, I recommend hire. Isn't it better to give someone a chance? The worst case is they get more training."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You should be stricter. We have high standards." (dismissive, doesn't address the underlying philosophy conflict)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "That's a generous instinct, and it comes from a good place. Here is the reality: our interview process is designed to have a 60-80% false positive rate at the individual interviewer level — meaning most candidates who get a 'hire' from one interviewer will be rejected by the HC. The system accounts for this by requiring multiple hire signals and HC calibration. If you always give the benefit of the doubt, your hire recommendations will be discounted by the HC. Worse, you will waste the HC's time resolving your false positives. The most useful interviewers are calibrated — they recommend hire only when they have strong evidence. A 'weak hire' from a calibrated interviewer is more valuable than a 'strong hire' from someone who always gives the benefit of the doubt. Strive to be calibrated, not generous."
adversarialResponse.pattern: calibration-over-generosity — explain the system's false positive rate and why calibrated feedback is more useful than generous feedback. The HC needs accurate signals, not kind ones.

sections.title: Calibration — How to Stay Consistent Across Candidates

description.value:
Calibration ensures that all interviewers evaluate candidates against the same bar. Without calibration, one interviewer's 'hire' is another interviewer's 'no hire' for the same performance. FAANG companies invest heavily in calibration because inconsistency in interviewing is the fastest way to degrade hiring quality.

How calibration works: (1) Calibration sessions: Regular meetings where interviewers review anonymized candidate feedback together. Each interviewer shares their recommendation and evidence. The group discusses whether the recommendation is consistent with the shared bar. (2) Shadowing: New interviewers shadow experienced interviewers, then co-interview with feedback, then interview solo with feedback, then are certified. This progression ensures consistency through apprenticeship. (3) Rubric review: Interview questions have rubrics that define what L4, L5, L6 performance looks like. Interviewers refer to these rubrics when writing feedback to stay calibrated. (4) Feedback audit: The hiring committee reviews interview feedback for consistency and flags interviewers who are outliers (too generous or too strict).

How to calibrate yourself: (1) Shadow 3-5 interviews before interviewing solo. Pay attention to how the experienced interviewer asks questions, probes for depth, and writes feedback. (2) Write practice feedback for 5-10 candidates you shadowed and compare your feedback to the experienced interviewer's. Where did you agree? Where did you differ? (3) Ask for feedback on your feedback. After your first 5 solo interviews, ask the hiring committee to review your feedback quality. (4) Participate in calibration sessions regularly. Your calibration drifts over time if you don't recalibrate.

sections.title: The Hiring Committee — How Leveling Decisions Are Made

description.value:
The hiring committee (HC) is a panel of 3-5 Staff+ engineers who review all candidate feedback and make the final hire/no-hire decision, including the level. The HC does not interview the candidate — they evaluate the interviewers' feedback. This creates a quality incentive: if your feedback is vague or inconsistent, the HC may reject a candidate who should have been hired, or hire a candidate who should have been rejected.

How HC meetings work: (1) The HC chair reads the candidate's packet aloud: resume, each interviewer's feedback, and any recruiter notes. (2) Each HC member asks clarifying questions: 'The coding interviewer said the candidate was L5, but the system design interviewer said L4. Was there a specific behavior that explains this discrepancy?' (3) The HC discusses the candidate holistically, weighting signals by reliability. A strong coding signal from a calibrated interviewer carries more weight than a weak system design signal from a new interviewer. (4) The HC votes: hire/no-hire and at what level. Most HCs require consensus (no strong dissent) or majority vote. (5) The decision is documented and sent to the recruiter.

How leveling works in the HC: (1) The candidate is leveled based on the highest signal they demonstrated consistently, not the average. If the candidate demonstrated L6 thinking in system design but L5 in coding and L5 in behavioral, they may be hired at L6 if the L6 signal is strong and consistent. (2) Down-leveling is common: the HC may hire at L5 even though the candidate interviewed for L6. This happens when the candidate demonstrates strengths but not at the expected level for L6. (3) Up-leveling is rare but possible: the HC may hire at L6 when the candidate interviewed for L5, if the candidate demonstrated L6 signals consistently across rounds.

Serving on the HC: After 12-24 months as a certified interviewer, you may be invited to join the HC. This is a significant career milestone. HC service exposes you to dozens of candidate packets per month, which dramatically improves your own interviewing skills. It also builds your reputation as someone who makes high-judgment decisions about talent. HC service is a strong signal for promotion to L7+.

sections.title: Evaluating SAP and Enterprise Candidates Fairly

description.value:
SAP and enterprise candidates bring valuable experience but often perform poorly in FAANG interviews because the evaluation criteria favor traditional CS backgrounds. Staff+ interviewers have a responsibility to evaluate enterprise candidates fairly — not by lowering the bar, but by recognizing that enterprise experience demonstrates the same skills in a different context.

Common biases against enterprise candidates: (1) 'Not real engineering' bias: 'SAP is just configuration, not software engineering.' This ignores the fact that SAP architects design complex distributed systems, manage massive data volumes, and solve real-world enterprise problems at scale. (2) 'Can't code' bias: 'ABAP is not a real programming language.' ABAP is a strongly-typed, object-oriented language with complex runtime characteristics. An engineer who mastered ABAP can learn any language. (3) 'No distributed systems' bias: 'Enterprise systems don't deal with distributed systems challenges.' Actually, enterprise systems deal with exactly these challenges — consistency, fault tolerance, scalability, security — in the context of 20-year-old legacy systems that are harder to work with than greenfield microservices. (4) 'Slow to adapt' bias: 'Enterprise engineers are used to slow, process-heavy environments.' This ignores the fact that enterprise engineers often manage complex migrations, critical incidents, and multi-team coordination under tight regulatory constraints.

How to evaluate fairly: (1) Translate the context. When a candidate says 'I led a global S/4HANA migration,' that is equivalent to 'I led a multi-year, multi-million dollar distributed system migration affecting 10,000+ users across 5 regions.' The scale and complexity are real. (2) Focus on fundamentals. Does the candidate understand distributed systems concepts, even if they explain them in enterprise terms? 'We used change data capture to synchronize data across systems' demonstrates the same understanding as 'we used CDC for data replication.' (3) Probe for depth. 'You mentioned SAP CPI for integrations. What were the tradeoffs you considered? Why CPI over custom REST APIs? What were the failure modes?' This tests the same depth as asking about a microservices architecture. (4) Distinguish between 'knows the technology' and 'understands the concept.' An enterprise candidate may not know Kubernetes but may deeply understand the deployment and scalability challenges that Kubernetes solves. Probe for conceptual understanding, not tool familiarity.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I adjust my evaluation for an enterprise/SAP candidate?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: Does the candidate demonstrate conceptual understanding of the underlying principles, even if their examples use enterprise-specific technologies?

branches.condition: Yes — they explain tradeoffs, failure modes, and design decisions in any technology context
branches.path: Evaluate normally. The candidate has transferable skills. Focus on depth of thinking, not technology choice. If they miss a FAANG-specific pattern (e.g., microservices), ask about their experience with the equivalent enterprise pattern (e.g., loose coupling via integration hub).

branches.condition: Partially — they know enterprise technologies well but struggle to abstract to general principles
branches.path: Probe with 'what if' questions that require abstraction. 'What if you couldn't use SAP CPI and had to build the integration yourself? What would you consider?' This tests whether the candidate can apply their knowledge outside the specific tool. If they can, they are likely a strong hire who needs ramp-up on FAANG-specific tools.

branches.condition: No — they only know one technology stack and cannot discuss principles outside it
branches.path: This is a risk. Evaluate whether their existing knowledge depth compensates for lack of breadth. An L4 hire who is strong in one area can learn. An L6 hire who cannot abstract is likely not ready. Recommend hire only if their specific knowledge maps directly to your team's needs.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The best enterprise candidates feel underrated in FAANG interviews. They know their work is harder than what FAANG interview questions represent, but they cannot demonstrate it because the questions are designed for a different context. As a Staff+ interviewer, your job is to recognize the signal through the noise. The enterprise candidate who can abstract their experience to general principles is often a stronger hire than a traditional CS candidate with the same interview performance, because the enterprise candidate has dealt with real-world complexity (regulation, legacy systems, multi-team coordination) that FAANG engineers often don't face.

description.value:
Unconscious bias in interviewing is well-documented and affects every interviewer. The goal is not to eliminate bias (which is impossible) but to mitigate its effects through structured processes and self-awareness.

Types of bias that affect FAANG interviews: (1) Similarity bias: You favor candidates who remind you of yourself (same background, same school, same interests). Mitigation: when you feel a strong positive reaction to a candidate, ask yourself: 'Would I feel the same if this candidate had a different background?' (2) Confirmation bias: You form an initial impression early and then seek evidence that confirms it while ignoring evidence that contradicts it. Mitigation: write down your initial impression after the first 5 minutes, then deliberately look for evidence that challenges it. (3) Halo effect: One strong signal (e.g., the candidate went to a top school) colors your evaluation of other signals. Mitigation: evaluate each signal independently. The candidate's school does not affect their coding ability. (4) Contrast effect: A candidate who interviews after a weak candidate seems stronger than they are, and vice versa. Mitigation: use the rubric, not your memory of previous candidates. Each candidate should be evaluated against the bar, not against the previous candidate.

Bias-mitigation practices: (1) Use structured interviews with consistent questions and rubrics. Unstructured interviews where interviewers 'follow their gut' produce the most biased outcomes. (2) Take notes during the interview, not after. Memory is biased — you remember things that confirm your impression and forget things that contradict it. (3) Separate evidence from interpretation. 'The candidate paused for 15 seconds before answering' is evidence. 'The candidate was nervous' is interpretation. Write evidence in your feedback. (4) Do not look at the candidate's resume before the interview. Forming pre-conceptions about the candidate's background biases your evaluation. Some FAANG companies enforce this by hiding resumes from interviewers.

sections.title: From SAP Engineer to FAANG Interviewer — A Progression Path

description.value:
The transition from SAP engineer to FAANG interviewer follows a predictable path. Understanding this path helps you set expectations and plan your progression.

Month 1-3: Learn the system. Attend an interviewer training session. Learn the company's interview philosophy, question design principles, and feedback format. Watch 3-5 mock interviews conducted by experienced interviewers.

Month 4-6: Shadow experienced interviewers. Observe real interviews. Take notes on question delivery, probing techniques, time management, and feedback writing. Debrief with the interviewer after each session. Ask: 'What did you see that I missed? What made you probe deeper on that topic?'

Month 7-12: Co-interview with feedback. Co-interview with a certified interviewer. You lead the interview while they observe and provide real-time feedback. After the interview, compare your feedback to theirs. Identify gaps in your calibration and probing depth.

Month 13-18: Solo interviews with feedback. Conduct solo interviews. Your feedback is reviewed by the HC for consistency and quality. Most interviewers need 5-10 solo interviews before their feedback is fully calibrated.

Month 19-24: Certification. After 10-20 solo interviews with consistent feedback quality and calibration, you are certified as a full interviewer. You can now design new interview questions, train new interviewers, and eventually serve on the HC.

Why this matters for SAP engineers: Becoming a certified FAANG interviewer is a powerful signal. It demonstrates that you have mastered the company's technical bar and are trusted to evaluate others against it. It also gives you deep insight into the interview process, which makes you a better candidate if you ever switch teams or companies. For SAP engineers, the interviewer certification is a concrete milestone that validates your transition from SAP expert to FAANG Staff+ engineer.

details: Staff+ Insight: Being an interviewer is not a burden you must endure. It is a leadership opportunity that shapes the organization's future. Every hire you make is a bet on the next generation of the company. A good hire multiplies the team's output. A bad hire costs years of productivity and cultural damage. The Staff+ interviewer who takes this responsibility seriously — who designs good questions, writes calibrated feedback, avoids bias, and evaluates candidates fairly — is one of the highest-leverage contributors in the organization. For SAP engineers, the journey from interviewee to interviewer is a powerful arc. It demonstrates the complete transition from SAP expert to FAANG Staff+ engineer — no longer just being evaluated, but evaluating others and shaping the culture.
